message:
fix(fnp-python): in-Rust ma_average for unweighted/no-return path; retain numpy fallback

crates/fnp-python/src/lib.rs replaces the `ma_average` pure passthrough
with a Rust-native fast path that handles the common shape
(unweighted, `returned=False`, scalar-or-None axis) without crossing
the numpy.ma call boundary. The slower / less-common shapes
(weighted, `returned=True`, tuple axis, non-numeric inputs) still
delegate to numpy.ma.average via a captured closure.

Fast-path flow:

1. Short-circuit to the numpy fallback if `returned=True` or
   `weights=Some(...)` — those paths aren't implemented in Rust yet.
2. Extract the input as a MaskedArray with `extract_numeric_masked_array`;
   non-numeric inputs trigger the numpy fallback.
3. Parse `axis` with `extract_axis_spec`; `None` (flat average),
   scalar (single-axis reduction) are handled in Rust; tuple axis falls
   back to numpy so numpy's canonical tuple-axis semantics are
   preserved.
4. Compute masked counts via `MaskedArray::count(axis)`; bail to
   numpy on error (preserves numpy's error surface verbatim).
5. Compute the masked sum and divide by the count (with the usual
   all-masked → masked-result semantics) then lift back to a numpy
   masked-array result.

The diff head (shown) shows the fallback-closure setup + the gating
logic; the remainder of the diff implements the sum/divide/mask-plumb
path and the return-shape construction. Closure captures use
`clone_ref` so the fallback can still be invoked if the Rust fast
path bails mid-stream without double-consuming Py<PyAny> handles.

Net effect: the common `np.ma.average(a)` and `np.ma.average(a, axis=0)`
calls now avoid the per-call numpy import + attribute lookup chain,
while the weighted / returned paths keep exact numpy parity.

Co-Authored-By: Claude <noreply@anthropic.com>
